name: Cast Iron Skillet
material: seasoned cast iron
rationale: Cast iron cooking surfaces have no fluoropolymer (PTFE) coating to scratch off or fume, eliminating PFAS exposure from worn nonstick pans.
caveats: leaches iron into acidic foods (a concern for those with hemochromatosis/iron overload)
not truly nonstick until well seasoned

name: Stainless Steel Skillet
material: 18/8 (304) food-grade stainless steel
rationale: Stainless steel cookware contains no PTFE/PFAS coating, so there is no fluoropolymer layer to degrade, scratch, or release fumes at high heat.
caveats: low-grade or damaged stainless can leach trace nickel/chromium, mainly into very acidic foods over long cooks
avoid metal-on-metal abrasion in damaged pans
residual_concerns: trace nickel/chromium leaching with acidic foods

name: Ceramic-Coated Cookware
material: sol-gel mineral/ceramic nonstick coating (PTFE/PFOA-free)
rationale: Ceramic nonstick uses a silica-based sol-gel coating rather than fluoropolymers, avoiding PTFE and PFAS while keeping easy-release cooking.
tradeoffs: coating wears out faster than PTFE (often 1-2 years)
needs gentle utensils and low-medium heat
higher per-use cost as it must be replaced more often
caveats: ceramic nonstick coatings degrade over ~1-2 years and lose nonstick performance
verify product is genuinely PFAS-free, as some 'ceramic' pans add fluoropolymer reinforcement
avoid overheating which can release nanoparticles

name: Glass Food Storage Container
material: borosilicate or tempered soda-lime glass
rationale: Glass is inert and non-porous, so it does not leach BPA, phthalates, or shed microplastics into food, even when heated or storing acidic/fatty foods.
tradeoffs: lids are often still plastic or silicone
caveats: plastic snap-on lids may still contact food — choose glass/silicone lids where possible
thermal shock can crack glass (avoid sudden temperature swings)

name: Stainless Steel Food Container
material: 18/8 (304) food-grade stainless steel
rationale: Stainless steel does not contain BPA or phthalates and does not shed microplastics, making it a durable, inert food-storage option.
caveats: avoid using with very acidic foods for prolonged periods to minimize trace metal leaching
confirm food-grade (304/316) rather than lower grades
residual_concerns: trace metal leaching with acidic foods

name: Stainless Steel Water Bottle
material: 18/8 (304) food-grade stainless steel
rationale: Stainless steel bottles contain no BPA and do not leach antimony or shed microplastics the way polycarbonate and single-use PET bottles can.
caveats: avoid aluminum bottles with unknown/epoxy liners (liners may contain BPA)
confirm liner-free 304/316 stainless

name: Glass Water Bottle
material: borosilicate or tempered soda-lime glass
rationale: Glass is fully inert, leaching no BPA, antimony, or microplastics into water regardless of temperature or storage time.
tradeoffs: usually sold with a silicone sleeve for protection
caveats: caps/lids are often plastic — choose stainless or silicone caps
not ideal for high-impact use (e.g. children, gym)

name: Wood Cutting Board
material: hardwood (maple, walnut, or bamboo)
rationale: Wood boards do not shed microplastics into food during cutting the way plastic boards do, and many woods have natural antimicrobial properties.
tradeoffs: can warp or crack if soaked
caveats: must be dried and maintained to prevent deep grooves harboring bacteria
some bamboo boards use formaldehyde-based glues — choose food-safe adhesives

name: Stainless Steel Kitchen Utensils
material: 18/8 (304) food-grade stainless steel
rationale: Stainless utensils do not melt, degrade, or shed microplastics into hot food the way plastic spatulas and spoons can.
tradeoffs: can scratch nonstick or delicate cookware
caveats: not suitable for use on ceramic/nonstick surfaces (use wood or silicone there)
confirm 304-grade for food contact

name: Beeswax Food Wrap
material: cotton infused with beeswax, jojoba oil, and tree resin
rationale: Beeswax wraps replace PVC cling film, avoiding the phthalate plasticizers (including DEHP) and vinyl chloride associated with flexible PVC food films.
tradeoffs: cannot be used with raw meat or in the microwave
caveats: not heat-safe
those with bee-product allergies should avoid
won't seal liquids well

name: Silicone Stretch Food Cover
material: food-grade platinum-cured silicone
rationale: Reusable silicone covers replace PVC cling film, avoiding the phthalate plasticizers and vinyl chloride monomer associated with flexible PVC.
tradeoffs: covers fit a limited range of container sizes
caveats: choose food-grade, preferably platinum-cured silicone
low-grade silicone may contain fillers — do a pinch test (white streaking indicates fillers)

name: Glass Electric Kettle
material: borosilicate glass body with stainless steel base/heating element
rationale: A glass kettle keeps boiling water in contact only with inert glass and stainless steel, avoiding BPA and microplastic release from plastic kettle bodies and windows.
caveats: confirm the lid, gauge, and water-line components are stainless/glass, not plastic
stainless base/element should be 304-grade
residual_concerns: minor plastic parts (lid seals, switch) may remain

name: Ceramic Dishware
material: lead-free, cadmium-free glazed ceramic/porcelain
rationale: Quality glazed ceramic avoids melamine and formaldehyde migration that can occur when melamine tableware contacts hot or acidic foods.
caveats: older, imported, or decorative/handmade ceramics can have lead or cadmium in glazes — use only ware certified lead- and cadmium-safe for food
avoid using cracked or worn glazes
residual_concerns: glaze lead/cadmium risk in uncertified ware

sources.title: Lead in Foods, Cosmetics, and Medicines / Ceramicware

name: Fabric Shower Curtain
material: polyester, cotton, hemp, or linen textile (PVC-free)
rationale: Fabric curtains replace PVC vinyl, eliminating the off-gassing of phthalates and VOCs (and the characteristic 'new shower curtain' smell) from flexible vinyl.
tradeoffs: may need a liner to stop water
caveats: avoid pairing with a PVC liner, which reintroduces the hazard — use a PEVA/EVA or fabric liner
some fabrics carry stain/water-repellent (PFAS) finishes — choose untreated

name: PEVA Shower Curtain Liner
material: PEVA (polyethylene-vinyl acetate), chlorine- and phthalate-free
rationale: PEVA is a non-chlorinated plastic that does not require phthalate plasticizers, so it off-gasses far fewer phthalates and VOCs than PVC vinyl.
tradeoffs: still a single-use-ish plastic that eventually wears out
can have a mild odor initially
caveats: PEVA is still a plastic, not a fully inert material — a fabric curtain is preferable where possible
ventilate when new
residual_concerns: PEVA is still plastic and may emit some VOCs

name: Paraben-Free Cosmetics
material: cosmetics preserved without parabens (e.g. using ethylhexylglycerin, sodium benzoate, or other alternative systems)
rationale: Paraben-free formulas avoid methyl-, propyl-, and butylparaben, which are endocrine-active preservatives that can be absorbed through skin.
tradeoffs: alternative preservatives can irritate some users
caveats: 'paraben-free' does not mean fragrance- or phthalate-free — read the full ingredient list
some alternative preservatives carry their own concerns

name: Fragrance-Free Personal Care
material: personal-care products formulated without added fragrance/parfum
rationale: Fragrance-free products avoid 'parfum' mixtures, which commonly carry undisclosed phthalates and allergens used as scent carriers and fixatives.
caveats: 'unscented' can still contain masking fragrance — look specifically for 'fragrance-free'
fragrance-free does not guarantee paraben-free

name: Plain Bar Soap
material: simple saponified vegetable/animal fats, fragrance-free
rationale: Plain soap cleans as effectively as antibacterial soap for everyday use without triclosan, which FDA banned from consumer hand soaps over safety and resistance concerns.
caveats: choose genuinely fragrance-free bars to also avoid phthalate-carrying fragrance
soap is for general hygiene, not a substitute for proper hand-washing technique

sources.title: Antibacterial Soap? You Can Skip It, Use Plain Soap and Water

name: Bamboo Toothbrush
material: bamboo handle with nylon or castor-bean bristles
rationale: A bamboo handle replaces molded plastic, reducing plastic waste and microplastic shedding from the handle over the brush's life.
tradeoffs: bristles are usually still nylon (not compostable)
handle can darken/soften if left wet
higher cost than basic plastic brushes
caveats: most 'biodegradable' bamboo brushes still have nylon bristles that must be removed before composting
store dry to prevent mold

name: Water-Based Nail Polish
material: water-based, low-VOC polish free of the 'toxic trio' (toluene, formaldehyde, DBP)
rationale: Water-based and '3-free/10-free' polishes avoid dibutyl phthalate, formaldehyde, and the high-VOC solvents found in conventional polish.
caveats: 'water-based' is not standardized — verify it is free of DBP, toluene, and formaldehyde
removal still may require some solvent

name: Natural Loofah Sponge
material: dried luffa gourd fiber
rationale: A natural plant loofah replaces the synthetic plastic-mesh pouf, eliminating the microplastics those shed during use and disposal.
tradeoffs: can harbor bacteria if not dried
caveats: must dry fully between uses and be replaced regularly to avoid bacterial/mold growth
compost at end of life rather than landfill

name: Vinegar & Baking Soda Cleaner
material: diluted white vinegar (acetic acid) and sodium bicarbonate
rationale: Simple vinegar/baking-soda cleaning avoids the synthetic fragrance (phthalate-carrying), VOC solvents, and triclosan-type antibacterials in many commercial sprays.
tradeoffs: less effective as a disinfectant than registered products
not suitable for stone/marble or some finishes
caveats: vinegar is not an EPA-registered disinfectant — do not rely on it where true disinfection is required (e.g. illness, raw-meat surfaces)
never mix vinegar with bleach (produces toxic chlorine gas)

name: Essential Oil Diffuser
material: water-based ultrasonic diffuser with pure essential oils
rationale: A diffuser avoids the phthalate-laden synthetic fragrance and VOC emissions of plug-in fresheners and the combustion byproducts of scented paraffin candles.
caveats: essential oils still emit VOCs/terpenes and can trigger asthma or irritate pets (cats are sensitive)
use sparingly and ventilate
keep oils away from children and pets
residual_concerns: essential oils themselves emit terpenes/VOCs and can be irritants

name: Wool Dryer Balls
material: 100% felted wool
rationale: Wool dryer balls reduce static and drying time without the synthetic fragrance and quaternary-ammonium/VOC coatings that dryer sheets deposit on fabric.
tradeoffs: can be noisy in the dryer
caveats: if scenting with essential oils, those reintroduce VOCs and potential irritants
those with wool/lanolin allergies should avoid

sources.title: Indoor Air Quality / Volatile Organic Compounds

name: Natural Latex Mattress
material: GOLS-certified natural rubber latex with wool fire barrier
rationale: Natural latex with a wool fire barrier avoids both the petrochemical VOC off-gassing of memory foam and the added halogenated/organophosphate flame retardants used to meet flammability standards.
caveats: latex allergy is a contraindication
verify 'natural latex' is not blended with synthetic latex or topped with polyurethane foam
wool barrier is the chemical-free way to pass flammability codes

name: Organic Cotton Bedding
material: GOTS-certified organic cotton
rationale: Organic cotton bedding avoids polyester's microplastic shedding and the antimony catalyst residue from PET, and certified-organic processing limits formaldehyde wrinkle-resist finishes.
tradeoffs: needs ironing for a crisp look
caveats: non-organic 'wrinkle-free'/'easy-care' cotton often carries formaldehyde resin finishes — choose GOTS-certified
dyes/finishes still vary by manufacturer

name: Linoleum Flooring
material: natural linoleum (linseed oil, cork/wood flour, jute backing)
rationale: True linoleum is made from natural materials and contains no PVC, so it avoids the phthalate plasticizers, vinyl chloride, and VOC off-gassing of vinyl/LVT flooring.
caveats: do not confuse natural linoleum (Marmoleum-type) with 'vinyl' or 'PVC' flooring marketed loosely as lino
adhesives used during install can be a separate VOC source — choose low-VOC

sources.title: Indoor Air Quality / Flooring and VOCs

name: Wool Area Rug
material: natural wool pile with natural-fiber (cotton/jute) backing
rationale: Wool rugs avoid the microplastic shedding of synthetic (nylon/polypropylene) carpet and the VOC/flame-retardant treatments and adhesives common in synthetic broadloom.
caveats: wool is naturally flame-resistant, but check that backing/pad is not a separate VOC or flame-retardant source
some 'wool blend' rugs still contain synthetic fiber

sources.title: Indoor Air Quality / Carpet and VOCs

name: Organic Cotton Clothing
material: GOTS-certified organic cotton
rationale: Organic cotton garments avoid the microplastic fibers polyester sheds in wash and wear, plus the antimony catalyst residue of PET; certified processing limits formaldehyde finishes.
caveats: wrinkle-resistant/no-iron cotton often uses formaldehyde resins — choose GOTS
performance/athletic wear still typically requires some synthetic

name: Glass Baby Bottle
material: borosilicate or tempered glass with silicone sleeve
rationale: Glass baby bottles are inert and do not leach BPA/BPS or shed microplastics into warmed milk/formula, a concern shown with heated and shaken plastic bottles.
caveats: nipples are still silicone (choose food-grade) — avoid latex if allergy concern
handle thermal shock carefully when warming

name: Silicone Teether
material: food-grade platinum-cured silicone
rationale: Silicone teethers replace soft PVC, eliminating the phthalate plasticizers (notably DEHP) that leach from flexible vinyl when chewed by infants.
caveats: confirm food-grade, BPA-free, platinum-cured silicone
inspect regularly for tears (choking risk)
avoid liquid/gel-filled teethers

name: Wool/Cotton Pajamas (Snug-Fit)
material: naturally flame-resistant wool or snug-fitting untreated cotton
rationale: Snug-fit cotton and naturally fire-resistant wool sleepwear meet flammability standards by fit/fiber rather than added halogenated or organophosphate flame retardants.
tradeoffs: snug-fit sizing required by regulation (looser styles need treatment)
caveats: in the US, loose-fitting children's sleepwear must be flame-retardant-treated by law — only snug-fit or wool styles legally avoid added retardants
verify 'untreated' on the label

name: Natural Wood Toys
material: solid wood with non-toxic, food-grade water-based finishes
rationale: Solid wood toys avoid soft PVC, removing the phthalate plasticizers and (in older/imported toys) lead/cadmium pigments associated with vinyl playthings.
tradeoffs: can splinter or crack if low quality
caveats: verify finishes/paints are non-toxic and lead-free (some imported painted wood toys have had lead pigments)
ensure no small parts for young children (choking)

sources.title: Phthalates and Lead in Children's Products

name: Digital Receipt
material: emailed/app-based electronic receipt (no paper)
rationale: Going paperless avoids handling thermal receipt paper, which is coated with BPA or BPS/BPF developers that transfer readily to skin on contact.
caveats: sharing contact info raises privacy/marketing considerations
if a paper receipt is unavoidable, avoid using hand sanitizer immediately before handling (increases dermal BPA absorption)

sources.title: Bisphenol A Restriction in Thermal Paper

name: Fragrance-Free Baby Wipes
material: water-based wipes without added fragrance, parabens, or formaldehyde-releasers
rationale: Fragrance-free, paraben-free wipes avoid the phthalate fragrance carriers and formaldehyde-releasing/paraben preservatives applied to sensitive infant skin.
tradeoffs: shorter shelf life for some preservative systems
caveats: check for formaldehyde-releasers (e.g. DMDM hydantoin, bronopol) even on 'sensitive' wipes
plain water + cloth is the lowest-exposure option of all

name: Natural-Fiber / FR-Free Sofa
material: wool or natural-fiber upholstery with FR-free foam (TB117-2013 label without added flame retardants)
rationale: Furniture labeled flame-retardant-free under TB117-2013, with natural-fiber covers, avoids the PBDE/organophosphate flame retardants and PFAS stain treatments that migrate into house dust.
caveats: look for the TB117-2013 tag stating 'contains NO added flame retardant chemicals'
avoid 'stain-resistant' (often PFAS) treatments — choose untreated and use washable covers

name: Non-Aerosol / Fragrance-Free Hair Styling
material: pump (non-aerosol) or fragrance-free styling products
rationale: Pump (non-aerosol) styling reduces inhalable propellant VOCs, and fragrance-free formulas avoid the phthalate carriers used in scented hairsprays.
caveats: pump products still contain solvents — ventilate when applying
'fragrance-free' is the key claim to avoid phthalate carriers

name: Fabric, Metal, or Wood Storage
material: cotton/canvas bins, metal baskets, or wood boxes
rationale: Natural-fiber, metal, or wood storage avoids the microplastic dust and plasticizer residues shed by aging plastic bins.
caveats: plastic bins are a low-priority swap — focus on food-contact and heated items first
metal/wood are the most durable long-term

name: Fresh, Dried, or Glass-Jarred Food
material: fresh/frozen/dried foods or those packed in glass jars or Tetra-style cartons
rationale: Choosing fresh, dried, or glass-jarred foods avoids the BPA/BPS epoxy linings of metal cans, the dominant dietary source of bisphenol exposure.
caveats: jar lids may have a thin liner — minor contact vs full can lining
dried beans/legumes are a cheap, zero-liner staple swap
residual_concerns: jar-lid liners are a minor residual

name: Reusable Filter / French Press
material: French press, stainless pour-over filter, or refillable stainless pod
rationale: Brewing through stainless/glass instead of plastic pods avoids forcing near-boiling water through plastic, which sheds microplastics into the cup.
caveats: refillable stainless pods fit most pod machines if you want to keep the appliance
paper pour-over filters are also plastic-free
